title: Installing Python Packages
text:
Installing JupyterLab
JupyterLab is an interactive environment for writing Python scripts. It is especially useful for data analysis workflows. You may also install JupyterLab with pip or Conda:

pip
pip install jupyterlab


Conda
conda install -c conda-forge jupyterlab
Once installed, you can open it with:
jupyter lab

title: Installing R Packages
text:
In R, you can install packages directly from within your script using the install.packages() function. For example, to install ggplot2, a popular package for data visualization, include the following line at the beginning of your script:
Before you install a package, make sure to set up your R environment with renv to manage dependencies and ensure reproducibility. See our page on renv for more details.

title: Installing R Packages
text:
Installing RStudio
To install RStudio, an interactive environment for writing R scripts, please refer to the installation instructions on the RStudio site.

title: Managing Software Environments
text:
Many genomics analyses rely on dozens of software packages, each with specific version requirements. Installing and updating these packages manually can lead to version conflicts, where one tool requires a different version of a dependency than another. This becomes especially problematic if you must run multiple analyses at once.
Environment management tools solve this problem by allowing you to create isolated software environments for each project. Each environment contains its own set of packages and versions, ensuring that different projects do not interfere with one another.
These tools also make analyses easier to reproduce and share. Environment details can be saved to files, allowing collaborators to recreate the exact software environment used in an analysis.

Below are two commonly used tools for managing environments in genomics workflows.




Conda for Python



renv for R

title: Git and GitHub
text:
GitHub is a web-based platform for hosting and sharing code. It is built on top of Git, a tool that tracks changes to files over time.
In practical terms, Git allows you to:
GitHub provides an online location where those tracked files can be stored, backed up, and shared with others.
Below we will provide a quick tutorial to Git and GitHub, highlighting the basic tools you should be familiar with. If you need further clarification or wish to learn about more advanced features (e.g. branching, merging), GitHub has excellent tutorials on their documentation site.

title: Git and GitHub
section: Why use GitHub for Omics Analyses?
text:
Why use GitHub for Omics Analyses?
Omics workflows are often complex, and there is rarely a single “correct” way to analyze a dataset. Even when working with the same raw data, researchers may make different — yet reasonable — choices at each processing step.
For example, to analyze scRNA-seq data:

Counts may be normalized using a simple log1p(CPM) transformation or a more sophisticated method such as scTransform.
Cells may be filtered using thresholds of 100 genes, 200 genes, or another cutoff entirely.
Differential expression may use a p-value threshold of 0.01 or 0.05.
Different software packages or parameter settings may be used for clustering or dimensionality reduction.

The number of reasonable analysis paths is enormous.
This complexity is further compounded by:

Differences in package versions
Changes in software dependencies
Random seeds used in stochastic algorithms
Differences in computing environments

As a result, even identical code can produce different — yet equally reasonable — results on different machines. Because of this, it can be difficult for others to evaluate, critique, or reproduce your analysis workflow unless the exact scripts and parameters are available.
For this reason, when publishing omics analyses, we strongly recommend saving your analysis scripts and configuration files to a public GitHub repository. Doing so allows other researchers to inspect your exact workflow and build upon your work.

title: Git and GitHub
section: What Should (and Should Not) Go on GitHub
text:
What Should (and Should Not) Go on GitHub
Before creating a repository, it is important to understand what belongs there.

Files to Put on GitHub

Analysis scripts (.R, .py, .sh)
Jupyter notebooks (.ipynb)
SLURM job scripts (.sbatch)
README files
Small configuration files
Environment files (environment.yml, requirements.txt)
Small processed summary tables



Files That Should NOT Go on GitHub

FASTQ files
BAM or CRAM files
Count matrices
Intermediate alignment outputs
Any file larger than ~100–200 MB

Large omics data files should remain on the HPC or in dedicated storage systems — not on GitHub.

title: Git and GitHub
text:
Creating a Repository

Step 1: Create a Repository on GitHub

Log in to GitHub.
Click New Repository.
Choose a repository name.
Leave it empty (do not initialize with a README).
Click Create Repository.

GitHub will display a repository URL, such as:
https://github.com/<git_username>/<project_name>.git


Step 2: Initialize Git Locally
Navigate to your project directory (on your local machine or HPC):
cd /path/to/project
Initialize Git:
git init
This creates a hidden .git directory that tracks changes.


Step 3: Connect to GitHub
Add the GitHub repository as a remote using the repository URL:
git remote add origin https://github.com/<git_username>/<project_name>.git

title: Git and GitHub
text:
Basic Git Workflow
You only need three core commands for everyday use: add, commit, and push. First, add selects the files whose changes you want Git to track. Next, commit records those changes as a snapshot in your local repository along with a message describing the update. Finally, push uploads those committed changes to the remote GitHub repository so they are saved online and visible to others.

Add Files
To add changes to a single file:
git add <filename>
To add all changes:
git add .


Commit Changes
git commit -m "Commit Description"
A commit message should briefly describe what changed.


Push to GitHub
git push -u origin main
After the first push, future pushes can be made with:
git push

title: Git and GitHub
text:
Using a .gitignore File
We usually keep all of our files for an analysis in a single project directory, and it is inconvenient to physically separate out the files that should be saved to GitHub and the ones which should not.
The .gitignore file tells Git which files it should not track. If you do not already have a text file called .gitignore in your repository, you should create one.
An example .gitignore file for a omics analysis project might look like this:
# Raw sequencing data
*.fastq
*.fastq.gz

# Alignment files
*.bam
*.bai
*.sam

# Large matrices
*.mtx
*.h5ad
*.rds

# Temporary files
*.log
*.tmp

# MacOS system files
.DS_Store
Here, the asterisk (*) is a wildcard that matches any filename with the specified extension. This .gitignore file tells Git to ignore all FASTQ files, BAM files, large matrices, temporary files, and MacOS system files. You can also use .gitignore to ignore specific directories (e.g. data/) or files with specific names (e.g. config.yaml).






Note.gitignore and Already Tracked Files



Note that if you have already added a file to Git (e.g. with git add .), it will be tracked even if it matches a pattern in .gitignore. To stop tracking a file that has already been added, you can use:
git rm --cached <filename>
This will remove the file from Git’s tracking but keep it on your local filesystem. After running this command, the file will be ignored in future commits.

title: Introduction to the Resnick High Performance Computing Center (HPC)
text:
A single genomics experiment can produce several gigabytes (or more) of raw sequencing data — often too large for a personal computer to store and process efficiently. For this reason, at some point in your analysis, you will likely need to use the Resnick High Performance Computing Center (HPC) at Caltech.
Here, we provide a tutorial emphasizing the use of the HPC for the analysis of genomics data. For a more general tutorial, please refer to the Caltech HPC documentation.

title: Introduction to the Resnick High Performance Computing Center (HPC)
text:
What is the HPC?
Unlike your personal computer, the HPC is a shared computational environment designed to support hundreds of users and thousands of jobs simultaneously.
Conceptually, you can think of the HPC as a large pool of compute nodes managed by a scheduler, which decides when and where your jobs run.
Using the HPC is very different from working on a laptop:

There is no graphical user interface (GUI)
Most work is done from the command line via SSH
Large computational tasks must be submitted to a job scheduler rather than run directly

You can access the HPC in two main ways:

The command-line
The web-based interactive portal

title: Introduction to the Resnick High Performance Computing Center (HPC)
text:
Storage on the Caltech HPC
When working on the HPC, it is important to understand how storage is organized and where different types of files should be stored. The Caltech HPC provides several storage locations, each designed for a specific purpose.

User Account Space — 50 GB
Located at /home/<username>, your home directory. This is your personal storage space and is intended for scripts, configuration files, and small datasets. Because this space is limited, it should not be used for storing large sequencing files or alignment outputs.
Group Space — 20 TB
Located at /resnick/groups/<groupname>. This space is shared among members of your research group and is appropriate for large genomics datasets, reference genomes, and processed results that need to be accessible to multiple lab members. If your group requires additional storage, your PI can request more space by contacting hpc@caltech.edu.
Scratch Space — 500 TB (total shared capacity)
Located at /resnick/scratch. Scratch space is intended for temporary storage of large files during active analyses. This space is accessible to all users, so if you wish to use the scratch space, you should create a personal directory (e.g., /resnick/scratch/<username>) to work within. Files that have not been accessed for 14 days are automatically purged, so important results must be moved to your home or group directory once your job is complete.

title: Introduction to the Resnick High Performance Computing Center (HPC)
section: Interfacing with HPC through the Command Line
text:
Interfacing with HPC through the Command Line






NoteLinux, the Shell, and Bash



Your command line interface (also called a terminal or shell) is a text-based interface that allows you to communicate directly with a computer’s operating system. The Caltech HPC runs the Linux operating system. Most users interact with Linux through a shell called Bash (Bourne Again SHell). To use the Caltech HPC, it is essential that you have a basic understanding of Bash and Bash scripting. If, for whatever reason, you are unfamiliar with or uncomfortable with Bash or Bash Scripting, please refer to our Bash tutorial.



Signing In
To sign into the HPC from the command line, open your terminal and type:
ssh username@login.hpc.caltech.edu
The command line will prompt you for your password. After you enter your password, enter 1 to verify your login with DUO two-factor identification.
When you log in from the terminal, you are connecting to a login node. Login nodes are shared machines intended for:

Editing files
Submitting jobs
Managing data
Loading software modules

You should not run large analyses directly on the login node. Instead, computational jobs must be submitted to a scheduler, which will run them on dedicated compute nodes.

title: Introduction to the Resnick High Performance Computing Center (HPC)
text:
Scheduling a Job with SLURM
The HPC is a shared resource. To ensure fair and efficient usage, computational jobs must be submitted to a scheduling system rather than executed immediately.
Caltech’s HPC uses Simple Linux Utility for Resource Management (SLURM) to:

Allocate CPUs, memory, and GPUs
Manage job queues
Schedule jobs based on priority and availability
Monitor running jobs
Handle parallel and distributed computing tasks

The means, instead of running a command or script directly, you will typically place your command inside a job submission script and submit it to SLURM. Below we provide a brief tutorial for basic SLURM usage. To see a complete example of a SLURM job script for running kb count, please refer to our SLURM example script.

Useful SLURM Commands

sbatch job_file.sbatch - submit your job to SLURM
squeue -u $USER - show your current running jobs
scancel jobid - kill a job given its job ID
sacct -l -u $USER -j jobid - show the run information for a job given its job ID (useful for adjusting your requested resource allocation)



Submitting a Bash Script.
On the HPC, computational jobs are typically submitted as Bash scripts. A Bash script is simply a text file containing a series of shell commands that are executed in order. Instead of typing commands interactively into the terminal, you place them into a script and submit the script to SLURM using sbatch. SLURM then schedules your job, allocates the requested resources (CPUs, memory, time), and runs the script on a compute node.
Submitting jobs as scripts has several advantages: - Your analysis is reproducible. - Resource requests are explicitly documented. - Long-running jobs can execute without keeping your terminal session open. - You can easily rerun or modify the job later.
A SLURM batch file begins with a series of lines starting with #SBATCH. These are instructions to SLURM. They are not regular comments — they control how your job is scheduled.
A few useful #SBATCH lines are:

#SBATCH --job-name=job_name Assigns a descriptive job name. The job name appears in squeue.
#SBATCH --account=mylab Charges the job to your lab’s HPC allocation. If you have access to multiple accounts, you must include this to ensure the proper account is charged. If ommited, your default account will be charged.
#SBATCH -p expansion Selects the partition. The Caltech HPC has two partitions: expansion (for general jobs) and GPU (for jobs requiring a GPU). Genome alignment does not currently have the functionality to be run on a GPU, so you must always choose expansion for alignment jobs.
#SBATCH --time=HH:MM:SS This sets the maximum runtime. If the job exceeds this time limit, SLURM will terminate it.
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8 Allocates 8 CPU cores (threads) to the job. By default, this values is set to 1. You may increase or decrease this depending on your analysis and current cluster availability. If you allocate multiple threads, make sure that the program you are running is configured to use the same number of threads.
#SBATCH --mem=20G Allocates 20GB of memory for the job. If your job fails with an out-of-memory error, increase this value. Note this setting controls RAM usage only — not disk storage.
#SBATCH --output=\path\to\output Specifies where standard output will be written. By default, SLURM creates a file named slurm-<jobid>.out in the working directory.
#SBATCH --error=\path\to\error Specifies where standard error messages will be written. By default, SLURM creates slurm-<jobid>.err in the working directory.
#SBATCH --chdir=\path\to\working_directory Sets the working directory for the job. By default, this is the directory from which you submit the job.

For any SLURM job file, we recommend specifying at minimum:

A descriptive job name
Your account
A maximum runtime
A memory allocation







Important



As a rule, you should read every #SBATCH line in your job script, understand what it means, and ensure that it is reasonable for your analysis.
Copying #SBATCH lines from templates, collaborators, or automated tools without verifying them can lead to serious mistakes — such as requesting a GPU partition for non-GPU software or reserving large amounts of memory or runtime unnecessarily. This can delay your job, waste allocation resources, and impact other HPC users.
If you need to rerun a job, use the sacct command to review the resources used previously and adjust your requested allocation accordingly.




SLURM Environment Variables
When a job is launched, SLURM automatically defines several environment variables based on your resource requests. These can be used inside your Bash script to make it more flexible and robust.
Two particularly useful variables are:

$SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK — the number of CPU cores allocated via #SBATCH --cpus-per-task
$SLURM_JOB_ID — the unique job ID automatically assigned by SLURM when the job is submitted

Using these variables allows your script to adapt automatically if you modify the #SBATCH directives.

title: Introduction to the Resnick High Performance Computing Center (HPC)
section: Interfacing with HPC through the Interactive Portal
text:
Interfacing with HPC through the Interactive Portal
If you prefer not to work exclusively from the command line, the HPC provides an Open OnDemand web portal.
From the Open OnDemand portal, you can:

Upload, download, move, and delete files through the browser
Edit and save files
Open a command-line shell
View up-to-date details of pending or running jobs
Submit jobs using preset templates
Launch JupyterLab, RStudio, or VS Code on a compute node

For interactive downstream analyses in Python or R, we recommend launching JupyterLab or RStudio through the portal.






NoteAccessing Group Storage from RStudio or JupyterLab



When launching RStudio or JupyterLab through the Open OnDemand portal, your session may be restricted to using your home directory as the working directory. In some cases, you may not be able to directly select your group directory (e.g., /resnick/groups/<groupname>) as the starting location.
If you wish to work with files stored in your group directory, you can create a symbolic link in your home directory:
ln -s /resnick/groups/<groupname> ~/group
This creates a shortcut called group in your home directory that points to your group storage space. You can then access your group files through this link inside RStudio or JupyterLab.

title: Introduction to the Resnick High Performance Computing Center (HPC)
section: Transferring Results to Your Local Machine
text:
Transferring Results to Your Local Machine
While raw sequencing files and intermediate alignment outputs are typically too large to process on a personal computer, final count matrices are often much smaller, often no larger than 2 GB. Sparse gene-by-cell matrices (e.g., .mtx, .h5ad, or .rds files) are frequently small enough to download and analyze locally.
If preferred, you may transfer these processed outputs to your personal machine using command-line tools such as scp or through the Open OnDemand web interface.
This workflow — heavy preprocessing on the HPC followed by downstream analysis locally — is common in genomics and helps reduce unnecessary use of shared HPC resources.






Important



Before downloading a file to your personal device, you should verify the file size with ls -lh. Then consider whether your local machine has sufficient, disk storage, RAM (system memory), and processing power to carry out the required downstream analyses. If a file is very large or if downstream analysis requires substantial memory, it is often better to continue working on the HPC.

title: Working from the Command Line
text:
Many genomics workflows are run from the command line, a text-based interface for interacting directly with your computer’s operating system.
Working from the command line allows you to:

run scientific software and analysis pipelines

organize and manipulate large datasets

automate repetitive tasks

interact with remote computing resources such as high-performance computing (HPC) clusters

Although graphical interfaces are useful for many tasks, command-line tools provide greater flexibility and control, and they are the standard way to run most bioinformatics software.
The exact commands available depend on your operating system. Most HPC systems use Linux, while personal computers commonly run Windows or macOS. Because macOS and Linux are both UNIX-based, they share the same command-line tools and conventions.

Select the tutorial that matches your operating system.




Linux / macOS (Bash / zsh)



Windows (PowerShell)

title: Experimental Design
text:
Modern omics experiments can generate enormous amounts of data, but the quality of the conclusions you can draw depends far more on the design of the experiment than on the sophistication of the analysis.
No amount of computational processing can recover information that was never measured, and poor experimental design can lead to misleading or uninterpretable results even when the data are analyzed correctly.
Before collecting or analyzing omics data, it is essential to think carefully about replication, confounding variables, and the structure of your experimental conditions.

title: Experimental Design
text:
Replication: Estimating Biological Variability
Biological measurements are not fixed values — they are samples drawn from an underlying distribution. Even genetically identical cells or organisms will exhibit variability due to biological noise, technical variation, and measurement error.
Importantly, biological variability is not simply “noise” in the sense of measurement error. In many omics experiments — particularly single-cell RNA-seq — we measure a biological system at a single moment in time. Each sample represents a snapshot of a dynamic process.
Cells may differ because of:

stochastic gene expression
cell cycle state
microenvironmental differences
developmental progression
temporal fluctuations
subtle regulatory dynamics

In single-cell experiments, this heterogeneity is often the very phenomenon we seek to understand.

Replication is Essential for Estimating Variability
To understand why replication matters, consider the following conceptual example:
Imagine measuring gene expression in two conditions: Control and Treated. Each condition has a true distribution of expression values, and those distributions overlap.

If you measure only one sample from each condition, you are observing just two points drawn from two overlapping distributions.

From those two points alone, you cannot estimate:

The variability within each condition
Whether the observed difference is typical or unusual
Whether the distributions truly differ

With only one replicate per condition, there is no way to distinguish biological signal from random fluctuation.
By contrast, when multiple biological replicates are included, you begin to estimate:

The spread of each distribution
The mean of each condition
The consistency of the effect


Replication does not simply increase sample size — it allows you to estimate variability, which is essential for meaningful statistical inference.
Importantly, biological replicates (independent samples) are what allow you to estimate biological variation. Technical replicates can assess measurement noise, but they do not substitute for biological replication.

title: Experimental Design
section: Orthogonality: Separating Biological Signal from Technical Effects
text:
Orthogonality: Separating Biological Signal from Technical Effects
In many omics experiments, multiple factors influence the data. These may include:

Biological condition (e.g., control vs treated)
Batch (e.g., sequencing run or processing day)
Donor identity
Time point
Library preparation method

A well-designed experiment ensures that these factors can be separated during analysis. This property is known as orthogonality.
An experimental design is orthogonal when each factor varies independently of the others in a balanced way.

A Confounded Design
Consider the folowing design:



Sample
Condition
Batch




S1
Control
A


S2
Control
A


S3
Treated
B


S4
Treated
B



Here, condition and batch are perfectly correlated. All control samples were processed in batch A, and all treated samples were processed in batch B.
If differences are observed between control and treated samples, it is impossible to determine whether they are due to biological condition or batch effects.
No statistical model can separate these effects, because they are structurally inseparable in the data.


An Orthogonal Design
Now consider a balanced alternative:



Sample
Condition
Batch




S1
Control
A


S2
Control
B


S3
Treated
A


S4
Treated
B



In this design:

Both conditions appear in both batches.
Batch and condition are not perfectly correlated.
The effects of condition and batch can be estimated independently.

This symmetry allows downstream statistical models to distinguish biological differences from technical variation.


Why Orthogonality Matters
Many omics analyses use statistical models that assume effects can be estimated independently. If your experimental factors are confounded, those assumptions are violated before analysis even begins.
Orthogonality does not require perfection, but it does require intentional balance. When designing experiments, ask:

Are conditions distributed across batches?
Are donors balanced across treatments?
Are time points evenly represented?

If one variable is systematically aligned with another, interpretation becomes difficult or impossible.

title: Experimental Design
section: Know What Your Experiment Can Answer
text:
Know What Your Experiment Can Answer
Every experimental design limits the questions you can ask:

Without replication, you cannot estimate variability
With confounded factors, you cannot separate effects
With unbalanced sampling, results may be biased

Thinking carefully about these constraints before data collection saves substantial time and prevents incorrect conclusions.

title: Experimental Design
section: Computational Analysis Cannot Fix Design Problems
text:
Computational Analysis Cannot Fix Design Problems
The tools described on this site assume that the input data were generated under a reasonable experimental design.
They can help correct technical variation, but they cannot recover missing structure or resolve fundamental confounding.
Strong experimental design is the foundation of meaningful omics analysis.

title: Analyzing Your 10x Chromium Single Cell 3’ scRNA-seq Assay Data
section: Step 2: Pseudoalign Your Library Reads with kb-python
text:
Step 2: Pseudoalign Your Library Reads with kb-python
Aligning reads from a 10x Genomics Library is relatively straightforward. After generating a reference index with kb ref, pseudoalign your reads to the reference with kb count.

An Example
Here we provide a brief example of how to pseudoalign a 10x library to a reference using kb count. For a more in-depth tutorial, see our page on kb-python.
To psuedoalign your scRNA-seq 10x data, run:
kb count --h5ad -x 10XV3 -g t2g.txt -i index.idx \
  R1.fastq.gz R2.fastq.gz
where index.idx and t2g.txt are files generated by kb ref, and R1.fastq.gz and R2.fastq.gz are your raw paired-end reads. The --h5ad argument outputs an .h5ad file suitable for downstream processing with Scanpy.
If you performed a single-nucleus RNA-seq experiment, instead use the NAC workflow to pseudoalign both nascent and mature reads:
kb count --h5ad -x 10XV3 --workflow=nac -g t2g.txt -i index.idx  \ 
  -c1 cdna.txt -c2 nascent.txt R1.fastq.gz R2.fastq.gz
Here, cdna.txt and nascent.text are files generated by kb ref --workflow=nac.






Important



This example is specifically for the 10x Chromium Single Cell Next Gem v3 chemistry. For the v4 chemistry, replace the technology string -x 10XV3 with -x 10XV4.

title: Welcome to CBRC Tutorials!
text: This site is designed to guide Caltech students and staff through the full process of working with omics data—from formatting and processing to analysis—using the equipment and experimental setups commonly found at Caltech. Whether you’re just getting started or looking to refine your workflow, these tutorials provide step-by-step instructions tailored to our unique environment.

title: Welcome to CBRC Tutorials!
text:
Before You Start

A Quick Note on Experimental Design
Although this site focuses on the computational side of omics experiments, it’s important to remember that good experimental design is crucial for successful data analysis. Without a well-designed experiment, even the best computational tools may not yield meaningful insights. To learn more about the principles of experimental design, we recommend checking out our page on experimental design and consulting with the CBRC team for guidance on your specific project.


Recommended Programming Tools and Resources
Nearly all modern genomics and bioinformatics tools are developed for R or Python, and all of our analysis workflows rely on libraries from one of these two ecosystems. We assume readers have basic familiarity with at least one of these languages and suggest performing analyses using RStudio (for R) or JupyterLab (for Python).
In addition to knowing R and Python, we also recommend becoming familiar with the following tools used to document, manage, and run analysis workflows:


Sharing Your Code on GitHub


Managing Software Environments


Working from the Command line


Using the HPC for Compute Intensive Tasks

title: Analyzing Your Parse Biosciences Evercode WT scRNA-seq Assay Data
section: Step 2: Pseudoalign Your Library Reads with kb-python
text:
Step 2: Pseudoalign Your Library Reads with kb-python
Aligning reads from a Parse Evercode WT library requires additional consideration compared to other scRNA-seq assays. Unlike most platforms, which use primers targeting a specific region of each mRNA molecule, Parse Evercode WT employs two types of primers — poly(T) primers that bind to the 3′ poly(A) tail and random oligo primers that bind to random internal regions of the transcript. Each primer type has its own corresponding set of cell-specific barcodes. As a result, each cell is represented by two barcodes rather than one, as in assays such as 10x Genomics.
To generate a unified count matrix from the raw reads, the counts associated with these two barcodes must be collapsed so that each cell is represented once. In kb-python, this is accomplished by replacing all random oligo barcodes with their corresponding poly(T) barcodes using the -r argument.






Important



When using -r to specify a replacement list in kb count, two count matrices will be produced: one with the original barcodes (stored in the output file counts_unfiltered) and one with the replacement barcodes (stored in the output file counts_unfiltered_modified).

title: Analyzing Your Parse Biosciences Evercode WT scRNA-seq Assay Data
text:
An Example (v2)
Here we provide a brief example of how to pseudoalign a Parse Evercode WT v2 library to a reference using kb count. For a more in-depth tutorial, see our page on kb-python.
To psuedoalign your Parse data, run:
kb count --h5ad -x SPLIT-SEQ -r v2_replace.txt \
  -g t2g.txt -i index.idx R1.fastq.gz R2.fastq.gz
where index.idx and t2g.txt are files generated by kb-ref, and R1.fastq.gz and R2.fastq.gz are your raw paired-end reads. The replace.txt file specifies the barcode substitution. The --h5ad argument generates an h5ad formatted file for downstream processing with Scanpy.
If you performed a single-nucleus RNA-seq experiment, instead use the NAC workflow to pseaudoalign both nascent and mature reads:
kb count --h5ad -r v2_replace.txt --workflow=nac -x 10XV3 -g t2g.txt -i index.idx \
  -c1 cdna.txt -c2 nascent.txt R1.fastq.gz R2.fastq.gz
Here, cdna.txt and nascent.text are files generated by kb ref -workflow=nac.
You can download the file v2_replace.txt for the v2 chemistry here.

title: Analyzing Your Parse Biosciences Evercode WT scRNA-seq Assay Data
text:
An Example (v3)
Currently, kb-python does not include a built-in specification for Parse Evercode WT v3. Instead, we must provide the assay information manually by specifying:

v3_onlist.txt — a list of valid cell barcodes
a technology string defining barcode positions within the read: -x "1,10,18,1,30,38,1,50,58:1,0,10:0,0,0"
v3_replace.txt - mapping between poly(T) and random oligo barcodes
the orientation of the first read (R1) with relative to your library specification: --strand=forward
the read parity (paired if both paired-end reads contain the biological sequence and single otherwise): --paired=single

Then, run:
kb count --h5ad --strand=forward --parity=single \
  -r v3_replace.txt -w v3_onlist.txt \
  -x "1,10,18,1,30,38,1,50,58:1,0,10:0,0,0 \
  -g t2g.txt -i index.idx R1.fastq.gz R2.fastq.gz

title: Analyzing Your Bulk RNA-seq Assay Data
section: Step 2: Pseudoalign Your Library Reads with kb-python
text:
Step 2: Pseudoalign Your Library Reads with kb-python
Here we provide a brief example of how to pseudoalign a bulk library to a reference using kb count. For a more in-depth tutorial, see our page on kb-python.






NotescRNA-seq vs Bulk RNA-seq with kb count



The default kb count command is designed for scRNA-seq data, where each captured RNA molecule is reverse transcribed at most once and labeled with a unique molecular identifier (UMI). These UMIs allow PCR duplicates to be identified and collapsed so that the final counts approximate the number of original RNA molecules captured from each cell.
This is not the case for bulk RNA-seq data. In bulk RNA-seq, RNA from an entire sample is extracted and fragmented before sequencing. As a result, a single RNA molecule can generate multiple sequencing reads, either because the transcript was fragmented into multiple pieces or because fragments were amplified during PCR. Since bulk RNA-seq libraries typically do not include cell barcodes or UMIs, individual reads cannot be directly traced back to their original molecules.
Instead, transcript abundances must be statistically inferred from the collection of sequenced fragments. In this case, pseudoalignment is used to determine which transcripts are compatible with each read. Then, transcript abundances are estimated using a statistical model while which for factors such as transcript length and shared sequences between transcripts.
For this reason, using kb count on bulk data requires special flags which generate a separate output than that for scRNA-seq.



An Example
To psuedoalign your bulk data, run:
kb count --h5ad -x BULK --tcc --matrix-to-directories --parity=paired -g t2g.txt -i index.idx \
  sample1_R1.fastq.gz sample1_R2.fastq.gz sample2_R1.fastq.gz sample2_R2.fastq.gz
where index.idx and t2g.txt are files generated by kb ref, and R1.fastq.gz and R2.fastq.gz are your raw paired-end reads. This will command will create a directory for each sample containing the abundance estimates for each transcript.
The --tcc flag with the --matrix-to-directories flag will create a directory for each sample (here one for sample 1 and one for sample 2). The directory names will begin with abundance and be numbered according to the order in which the samples were listed in the command.
Assuming paired-end sequencing, we also include the flag --parity=paired to indicate that both read 1 and read 2 for each read contain biological sequence.
If you performed a bulk nuclear RNA-seq experiment, generate your index with kb ref using the NAC workflow. Then, run kb count with the standard workflow as shown above.

text:

title: The Command Line, Linux, and Bash
text:
Your command line or terminal (also called a shell) is a text-based interface that allows you to communicate directly with a computer’s operating system.
Nearly all servers, including the Caltech HPC, run the Linux operating system. Most users interact with Linux through a shell called Bash (Bourne Again SHell).

title: The Command Line, Linux, and Bash
text:
Bash Commands
Regardless of your operating system or command-line interpreter, a command has the same general structure:
command [options] [arguments]
For example, a standard kb-python command might look like:
kb -x 10XV4 read1.fastq.gz read2.fastq.gz
In this case:

kb is the command (the program being executed)
-x 10XV4 is an option (or flag) modifying how the program runs
read1.fastq.gz and read2.fastq.gz are inputs specifying file paths

title: The Command Line, Linux, and Bash
section: Understanding Paths and the Linux Filesystem
text:
Understanding Paths and the Linux Filesystem
A file path is a string that specifies the location of a file or directory within a filesystem.
Linux organizes files in a hierarchical directory structure that begins at a single top-level directory called the root, written as /. Every file and directory on the system exists somewhere under this root directory.
When you log onto a Linux machine (such as the HPC), you are placed in your home directory, typically /home/<username>. This directory is also represented by the shortcut ~.

Absolute vs Relative Paths
There are two types of paths:

Absolute paths start from the root directory and specify the full path to a file. For example: /home/user/my_project/data/file.txt
Relative paths specify the location of a file relative to the current working directory. For example, if your current working directory is /home/user/my_project, you can reference the same file with the relative path data/file.txt.

Whether you choose to use the relative or absolute path depends on your current working directory and the context of your command. Absolute paths are unambiguous and will always point to the same file, while relative paths can be more concise but depend on your current location in the filesystem.

title: The Command Line, Linux, and Bash
section: Essential Bash Commands for HPC Users
text:
Essential Bash Commands for HPC Users
If you plan to work through the command line, here are the minimum commands you should be comfortable with:
Navigating the Filesystem

pwd — Print your current working directory
ls — List files in the current directory
ls -lh — List files with human-readable sizes
cd directory_name — Change directories
cd .. — Move up one directory

Managing Files

mv file newname — Move or rename a file
cp file destination — Copy a file
rm file — Remove a file (permanently)
mkdir new_directory — Create a directory

Viewing Large Files
Genomics files can be tens or hundreds of gigabytes. You should never try to open them in a graphical editor.
Instead, use:

less file.txt — Scroll through a file
head file.txt — View the first 10 lines
tail file.txt — View the last 10 lines

These commands can be useful for quickly checking large FASTQ and SAM files.






ImportantViewing Compressed Files



Because genomics datasets are so large, most sequencing files (e.g., FASTQ files) are stored in compressed format, typically indicated by the .gz extension. Compression reduces storage space but means the file is not directly human-readable.
To inspect a compressed file without fully decompressing it, you can stream its contents using zcat and pipe the output to another command:

zcat file.txt | less
zcat file.txt | head
zcat file.txt | tail

This allows you to quickly inspect large compressed files without creating an uncompressed copy, which could consume significant storage space.


Editing and Creating Files
nano filename — Edit/create a file in the terminal
Display Text
echo "Hello, World!" — Print text to the terminal

title: The Command Line, Linux, and Bash
text:
An Introduction to Bash Scripting
A Bash script is a plain text file, typically with a .sh extension, containing a sequence of shell commands that are executed in order. Instead of typing commands interactively into the terminal, you place them in a file and run the file as a program. You can use Bash scripts to automate repetitive tasks, run complex workflows, and submit jobs to an HPC cluster.
A Bash script typically begins with:
#!/bin/bash
This line (called a shebang) tells the system to execute the script using the Bash shell.
You can run a Bash script from the command line with:
bash script.sh

Exit Status
Every command in Bash returns an exit status, which is a number indicating whether the command succeeded or failed. By convention, an exit status of 0 means success, while any non-zero value indicates an error. You can check the exit status of the last command with:
echo $?
This is useful for debugging and for writing scripts that can handle errors gracefully. You can manually set the exit status of a script with the exit command. For example, exit 1 will cause the script to terminate with an error status.


Variables
Variables allow you to store values (such as file paths or parameters) and reuse them throughout your script.
Say you want to define a variable for your project directory:
DIR=/home/username/project
Note that there should be no spaces around the equals sign. To use the variable, prepend $.
For example, to change to the project directory, you would use:
cd $DIR
It is good practice to wrap variables in quotes:
cd "$DIR"
This prevents issues if the variable contains spaces or special characters.
Environment Variables
Environment variables are predefined variables set by the shell or by systems like SLURM. They provide information about the system and the job environment. For example, when you submit a job array in SLURM, it creates environment variables like SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID that indicate which task in the array is currently running. You can access these variables in your script just like any other variable.
Some common linux environment variables include:

HOME - Your home directory
USER - Your username
PATH - A list of directories where the system looks for executable files
PWD - Your current working directory

Arrays
An array stores multiple values in a single variable. To define an array in Bash, you can use the following syntax:
ARRAY=( "value1" "value2" "value3" )
You can access individual elements of the array using their index (starting from 0):
echo "${ARRAY[0]}"  # Outputs: value1
echo "${ARRAY[1]}"  # Outputs: value2
echo "${ARRAY[2]}"  # Outputs: value3
If Statements
If statements allow you to execute different commands based on certain conditions. The basic syntax is:
if [ condition ]; then
  # commands to execute if condition is true
elif [ another_condition ]; then
  # commands to execute if another_condition is true
else
  # commands to execute if none of the above conditions are true
fi
For example, to check if a file exists before running a command, you use:
if [ ! -f "$FILE" ]; then
    echo "File exists and is not empty."
else
    echo "File is missing or empty."
    exit 1
fi
For Loops
For loops allow you to repeatedly execute a block of code for each item in a list. A for loop has the basice structure:
for item in list; do
  # commands to execute for each item
done
For example, to loop through a list of samples and print their names, you can use:
SAMPLES=( "S1" "S2" "S3" )
for SAMPLE in "${SAMPLES[@]}"; do
    echo "Processing sample: $SAMPLE"
done
You can also loop through files in a directory with:
for FILE in /path/to/directory/*; do
    echo "Processing file: $FILE"
done
or loop through all FASTQ files in a directory with:
for FILE in /path/to/directory/*.fastq.gz; do
    echo "Processing file: $FILE"
done
Strict Mode
When writing Bash scripts, it is good practice to enable strict mode at the beginning of your script. This command tells bash to exit immediately if any command returns a non-zero exit status (i.e., an error occurs). You can enable strict mode with the following line:
set -euo pipefail

title: The Windows Command Line
text:
Your command line (also called a terminal or shell) is a text-based interface that allows you to interact directly with your computer’s operating system.
While most HPC systems run Linux, many users perform preliminary analysis and data management on Windows machines. Windows provides command-line environments such as:
Both allow you to navigate the filesystem, manipulate files, and run software from the command line.
Even if most of your analysis is performed using Python or R, basic command-line skills are extremely useful when organizing files, running pipelines, or transferring data to an HPC system.

title: The Windows Command Line
text:
Command Structure
Regardless of your operating system or command-line interpreter, a command has the same general structure:
command [options] [arguments]
For example, a standard kb-python command might look like:
kb -x 10XV4 read1.fastq.gz read2.fastq.gz
In this case:

kb is the command (the program being executed)
-x 10XV4 is an option (or flag) modifying how the program runs
read1.fastq.gz and read2.fastq.gz are inputs specifying file paths

title: The Windows Command Line
section: Understanding Paths and the Windows Filesystem
text:
Understanding Paths and the Windows Filesystem
A file path specifies the location of a file or directory within the filesystem.
Windows organizes files using drive letters such as:
C:\
D:\
Most user files are stored in the Users directory:
C:\Users\username

Absolute vs Relative Paths
There are two types of paths: absolute and relative.
Absolute paths
An absolute path specifies the complete location of a file from the root of the drive.
C:\Users\user\project\data\file.txt
Relative paths
A relative path specifies a file relative to your current directory.
For example, if your current directory is:
C:\Users\user\project
you could reference the same file as:
data\file.txt
Absolute paths are always unambiguous, while relative paths depend on your current working directory.

title: The Windows Command Line
text:
Essential Windows Command Line Commands
If you plan to work from the command line, here are the most important commands to know.

Navigating the Filesystem

cd directory_name — Change directories
cd .. — Move up one directory
cd \ — Go to the root of the drive
dir — List files in the current directory
pwd (PowerShell) — Print the current working directory

For example, to navigate to the directory Documents and list the file in Documents:
cd Documents
dir


Managing Files

copy file destination — Copy a file
move file destination — Move or rename a file
del file — Delete a file
mkdir new_directory — Create a directory
rmdir directory_name — Remove a directory

For example, to create a directory project within your current directory and move a file called data.txt from your current directory to project:
mkdir project
move data.txt project


Viewing Files
Omics datasets can be extremely large. You should avoid opening large files in graphical editors.
Instead, use command-line tools to inspect them.

type file.txt — Print the contents of a file
more file.txt — Scroll through a file page-by-page

In PowerShell, you can also use:

Get-Content file.txt

This allows you to inspect files such as configuration files, logs, or small metadata tables.
Viewing the Beginning or End of Files (PowerShell)
To view the first lines of a file:
Get-Content file.txt -Head 10
To view the last lines:
Get-Content file.txt -Tail 10
These are useful for quickly checking large output files or logs.






ImportantCompressed Files in Genomics



Omics datasets are often stored in compressed formats to reduce storage requirements. These commonly include files ending in:
.gz
.zip
.tar.gz
Windows can extract many compressed formats using built-in tools or utilities such as 7-Zip.
For example, to extract a ZIP archive using the command line:
Expand-Archive file.zip
When working with large sequencing files, it is often best to inspect them on a Linux or HPC system, where tools like zcat and less allow compressed files to be streamed without full decompression.




Creating and Editing Files
Windows command lines are not ideal for editing large files, but you can create or open files using simple programs.
To open a file in Notepad:
notepad filename.txt
If the file does not exist, Notepad will prompt you to create it.


Displaying Text
You can print text to the terminal using:
echo Hello World
This is often used to verify commands or display messages.

title: An Example SLURM File: Running kb count
text:
Let us consider a hypothetical scRNA-seq experiment with four samples, each processed using the 10X Chromium GEM-X v4 chemistry and sequenced using two sequencing lanes. We want to run kb count on each sample, and we have already generated the necessary index files using kb ref. Below is an example SLURM job script that we could use to run kb count for all four samples in a single job array. The following tutorial assumes that you have a basic understanding of SLURM and how to submit jobs, as covered in our Intro to HPC. If you are new to SLURM, we recommend reviewing that tutorial before proceeding with this example.
We will be working under the assumption that our files are organized within the following working tree:
In this example:
It is generally good practice to organize your files by project and store your SLURM files within the project directory.

title: An Example SLURM File: Running kb count
text:
Example SLURM File
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=kb_example
#SBATCH --account=<groupname>
#SBATCH -p expansion
#SBATCH --time=06:00:00
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --mem=20G
#SBATCH --array=0-3%2
#SBATCH --chdir=/resnick/groups/<username>/my_project
#SBATCH --output=logs/%x_%A_%a.log
#SBATCH --error=logs/%x_%A_%a.err

set -euo pipefail

# ---- software environment ----
# Adjust this path if your conda install lives elsewhere
source ~/miniconda3/etc/profile.d/conda.sh
conda activate kb-python

# ---- project directories ----
FASTQ_DIR="fastqs"
OUT_DIR="kb_out"
INDEX_DIR="${OUT_DIR}/index"

IDX="${INDEX_DIR}/index.idx"
T2G="${INDEX_DIR}/t2g.txt"

# ---- sample list for the job array ----
SAMPLES=( "S1" "S2" "S3" "S4" )
SAMPLE="${SAMPLES[$SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID]}"

# ---- FASTQ inputs (example: two lanes) ----
L1R1="${FASTQ_DIR}/${SAMPLE}_L001_R1_001.fastq.gz"
L1R2="${FASTQ_DIR}/${SAMPLE}_L001_R2_001.fastq.gz"
L2R1="${FASTQ_DIR}/${SAMPLE}_L002_R1_001.fastq.gz"
L2R2="${FASTQ_DIR}/${SAMPLE}_L002_R2_001.fastq.gz"

KB_OUT_DIR="${OUT_DIR}/${SAMPLE}"
mkdir -p "${KB_OUT_DIR}"

# ---- check that all input files are present ----
for f in "${L1R1}" "${L1R2}" "${L2R1}" "${L2R2}" "${IDX}" "${T2G}"; do
  [[ -s "$f" ]] || { echo "Missing file: $f" >&2; exit 1; }
done

# ---- run kb-python ----
srun kb count \
  -i "${IDX}" \
  -g "${T2G}" \
  -x 10XV4 \
  -o "${KB_OUT_DIR}" \
  --h5ad \
  -t "${SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK}" \
  "${L1R1}" "${L1R2}" "${L2R1}" "${L2R2}"

Resource Request Summary
This SLURM script requests:

8 CPU cores
20 GB of RAM
A maximum runtime of 6 hours
Execution on the expansion partition
Charges to the  account
Execution within the my_project directory
Logging of standard output and error to the logs/ directory named according to the job name %x, job ID %A, and array index %a.

We also use the directive:
#SBATCH --array=0-3%2
This line tells SLURM to create four copies of the job (indexed from 0 to 3) in what is called a “job array”. A job array allows us to run the same script for multiple samples without having to create separate job files for each sample. Note that each job in the array will be allocated its own set of resources (8 CPUs, 20 GB RAM, etc.) as specified in the script. We can access the index of the current job in the array using the environment variable SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID, which we use to select the appropriate sample from our SAMPLES array.
We use the %2 part to limit the number of concurrent jobs to 2, which means that at most 2 jobs from the array will run at the same time. This can help manage resource usage and prevent overloading the system. For kb count, which can be resource-intensive, we don’t recommend running more than 2–4 jobs concurrently.
Why These Resources? The resource requests in this script are based on the expected computational requirements of running kb count on a single sample. The kb count command can be computationally intensive, particularly for large datasets, and benefits from parallel processing.
We request 8 CPU cores so that kb count can take advantage of multithreading, which can significantly reduce runtime. In practice, using 8–16 threads is often sufficient for most single-cell datasets.
The 20 GB memory allocation is a conservative estimate intended to ensure the job has enough RAM. In many cases, kb count uses approximately 5–10 GB of memory for a single sample, but requesting additional memory provides a safety margin and accounts for system overhead.
The 6-hour runtime is also a conservative estimate based on typical runtimes for similar datasets. Actual runtime depends on factors such as input size, sequencing depth, and cluster performance.
When requesting resources, it is important to strike a balance. Underestimating resource needs can cause jobs to fail, while overestimating them may increase queue wait times and reduce overall cluster efficiency. As you gain experience, you can use tools such as sacct to review the resource usage of completed jobs and adjust your requests accordingly.


Script Set-Up
Before running the analysis itself, the script includes a small setup section designed to make the workflow more robust and reproducible. These steps are not strictly required, but they are strongly recommended for reliable HPC usage.
First, we use set -euo pipefail to ensure that the script exits immediately if any command fails, if we try to use an undefined variable, or if any part of a pipeline fails. This helps catch errors early and prevents the script from continuing in an inconsistent state.
Next, we activate the software environment:
# Direct the script to the location of your conda installation. Adjust this path if your conda install lives elsewhere.
source ~/miniconda3/etc/profile.d/conda.sh
# Activate your environment containing kb-python. Here we assume the environment is named "kb-python".
conda activate kb-python
This loads the Conda environment containing kb-python and its dependencies. Using Conda environments is considered best practice because it ensures that:

the correct software versions are used
dependencies are consistent
analyses remain reproducible
scripts behave the same across systems

Without an explicitly defined environment, differences in software versions or system configurations can lead to unexpected behavior or inconsistent results. If you are unfamiliar with Conda, see our Conda Tutorial.
Altogether, these setup steps help ensure that your SLURM job runs smoothly and produces reliable results.


Defining Path Variables
Now we arrive at the section of the script where we define variables for our file paths. This is a critical step in writing HPC scripts, as it promotes readability and makes it easier to modify the script if your project structure changes.
FASTQ_DIR="fastqs"                ## Directory containing FASTQ files
OUT_DIR="kb_out"                  ## Directory to store kb-python outputs
INDEX_DIR="${OUT_DIR}/index"      ## Directory containing the pre-built index and t2g files
IDX="${INDEX_DIR}/index.idx"      ## Path to the pre-built kallisto index file
T2G="${INDEX_DIR}/t2g.txt"        ## Path to the transcript-to-gene mapping file 
The script then defines the sample list:
SAMPLES=( "S1" "S2" "S3" "S4" )
SAMPLE="${SAMPLES[$SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID]}"
Here, the Bash array provides the mapping between SLURM’s job array index and your sample names. Each job selects a single sample based on its assigned array index, allowing the same script to process multiple datasets automatically.
Based on the sample name, we then define the paths to the corresponding FASTQ files for each sample. In this example, we assume that each sample has two sequencing lanes, resulting in four FASTQ files per sample (R1 and R2 for each lane):
L1R1="${FASTQ_DIR}/${SAMPLE}_L001_R1_001.fastq.gz"
L1R2="${FASTQ_DIR}/${SAMPLE}_L001_R2_001.fastq.gz"
L2R1="${FASTQ_DIR}/${SAMPLE}_L002_R1_001.fastq.gz"
L2R2="${FASTQ_DIR}/${SAMPLE}_L002_R2_001.fastq.gz"


Creating Output Directories
Once the core directories are defined, the script constructs the expected FASTQ filenames and prepares the output directory:
KB_OUT_DIR="${OUT_DIR}/${SAMPLE}"
mkdir -p "${KB_OUT_DIR}"
Creating output directories explicitly is good practice because it prevents jobs from failing due to missing folders. The -p option ensures the command succeeds even if the directory already exists.


Checking Input Files
Before launching a computationally expensive job, the script verifies that all required files are present:
for f in "${L1R1}" "${L1R2}" "${L2R1}" "${L2R2}" "${IDX}" "${T2G}"; do
  [[ -s "$f" ]] || { echo "Missing file: $f" >&2; exit 1; }
done
This is an important safeguard. HPC jobs can run for hours, and a simple typo in a file path can otherwise lead to wasted compute time or incomplete results. By checking that each file exists and is non-empty, the script fails immediately if something is wrong.


Running kb count
The final line executes the analysis:
srun kb count ...
The srun command launches the program within the resources allocated by SLURM. The arguments passed to kb count specify the index, transcript-to-gene mapping, sequencing technology, and output directory.
Notably, the script uses:
-t "${SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK}"
This ensures that kb-python uses exactly the number of threads requested in the SLURM header. This pattern makes the script flexible — if you change the resource request, the program automatically adapts.

title: An Example SLURM File: Running kb count
section: Adapting This Script for Your Own Data
text:
Adapting This Script for Your Own Data
Although this example uses a specific directory structure, dataset, and analysis tool, the general principles can be applied to any HPC job script. When adapting this script for your own data, consider the following:

Resource Requests: Adjust the CPU, memory, and time requests based on the expected requirements of your analysis. Use past job data to inform these decisions with tools like sacct.
Directory Structure: Define path variables at the top of your script to reflect your project’s organization. This makes it easier to modify paths if your structure changes.
Sample List: Use a Bash array to manage your sample names and link them to SLURM array indices. This allows you to run the same script for multiple samples without duplication.
Input Validation: Always include checks to ensure that required files are present before running the analysis. This can save time and prevent errors.

Though optional, we also recommend setting up a software environment and activating it within your script. This promotes reproducibility and ensures that your analysis runs with the correct software versions and dependencies.

title: Managing Project Environments with renv
text:
renv is an R package that provides project-local dependency management for R. It helps you:

Keep each project’s R package versions isolated from other projects on your system.
Reproduce analyses by recording exact package versions in a lockfile (renv.lock).
Share a project with collaborators (or re-run it later) by restoring the same package set.

This is especially helpful for genomics workflows in R (e.g., Seurat, Bioconductor, edgeR), where package version differences can subtly change results.






Noterenv vs conda (R vs Python origins)



renv was introduced for R project reproducibility. Conda was introduced largely in the Python ecosystem (but can also manage R and many non-Python tools).
In mixed R + Python projects, a common pattern is:

Use renv to lock down R packages for the R portion of the analysis, and
Use conda (or another Python environment manager) for Python dependencies (often accessed from R via reticulate).

To learn more about conda, see our conda tutorial.

title: Managing Project Environments with renv
text:
renv is an R package that provides project-local dependency management for R. It helps you:

Keep each project’s R package versions isolated from other projects on your system.
Reproduce analyses by recording exact package versions in a lockfile (renv.lock).
Share a project with collaborators (or re-run it later) by restoring the same package set.

This is especially helpful for genomics workflows in R (e.g., Seurat, Bioconductor, edgeR), where package version differences can subtly change results.






Noterenv vs conda (R vs Python origins)



renv was introduced for R project reproducibility. Conda was introduced largely in the Python ecosystem (but can also manage R and many non-Python tools).
In mixed R + Python projects, a common pattern is:

Use renv to lock down R packages for the R portion of the analysis, and
Use conda (or another Python environment manager) for Python dependencies (often accessed from R via reticulate).

To learn more about conda, see our conda tutorial.

title: Managing Project Environments with renv
text:
Create a New Environment
With renv, each project directory becomes its own environment. The “environment” is defined by the packages recorded in renv.lock, and the packages are installed into a project-local library.

Open (or create) an R project folder, ideally as an RStudio Project.
Install renv (once per machine; you can do this in any R session):

install.packages("renv")

Initialize renv in your project (run inside the project directory):

renv::init()
This will:

Create a renv/ folder,
Create a lockfile renv.lock, and
Set up your project so renv activates when you open it.







NoteBest practice



Use one renv project per analysis (or per paper). This prevents package version drift and makes it much easier to rerun analyses later.

title: Managing Project Environments with renv
text:
Installing a Package with renv
Once renv is initialized, install packages as you normally would:
install.packages("Seurat")
or install from Bioconductor:
if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("BiocManager")
}
BiocManager::install("edgeR")
After installing (or updating) packages, record the state of the project library in the lockfile:
renv::snapshot()
That updates renv.lock so others (and future you) can restore the same package versions.

title: Managing Project Environments with renv
text:
Recreate an Old Environment
If you receive a project that already contains renv.lock, you can recreate the exact package library on your machine by running (in that project directory):
renv::restore()
This reads renv.lock and installs the recorded package versions into the project-local library.

title: Managing Project Environments with renv
text:
Using renv with RStudio
Most users run R genomics workflows in RStudio, and renv integrates cleanly:

Recommended workflow

Create an RStudio Project for your analysis folder (File → New Project…).
Run renv::init() once.
Install packages as needed.
Run renv::snapshot() whenever you want to “lock in” the working set of packages (often after a milestone).



What you should see in RStudio

renv usually activates automatically when you open the project.
If it doesn’t, run:

renv::activate()


Sanity checks
Confirm that you are using the project library:
.libPaths()
renv::status()


Troubleshooting
If you update R, change system libraries, or switch machines, and packages start failing to load:

Run renv::restore() again, and
Restart R (Session → Restart R) to ensure the project library is reloaded cleanly.

title: Creating a Conda Environment
text:
Conda is an open-source package and environment management system that simplifies software installation and version control. With Conda, you can:

Install and manage software (including Python and R packages) along with all their dependencies
Create isolated environments so different projects can use different versions of the same software without conflicts.
Reproduce analyses easily since an environment file (environment.yml) can capture the exact software versions used in your workflow.

This makes conda especially useful for computational biology and single-cell RNA-seq analysis where software dependencies can be complex and version-sensitive.






NoteConda vs renv (Python vs R origins)



Although Conda was originally popularized for Python workflows, it can also manage R environments (and many non-Python tools).
However, for R-specific projects, many users prefer renv for its tight integration with R and CRAN/Bioconductor packages.
In mixed R + Python projects, a common pattern is:

Use renv to lock down R packages for the R portion of the analysis, and
Use conda (or another Python environment manager) for Python dependencies (often accessed from R via reticulate). If your workflow includes both R and Python components, you may find it helpful to use both tools in tandem. To learn more about renv, see our renv tutorial.









NoteInstalling Conda



To install Conda, follow the Miniconda installation instructions.
Miniconda is the lightweight version of Anaconda and is recommended for most users.

title: Creating a Conda Environment
text:
Conda is an open-source package and environment management system that simplifies software installation and version control. With Conda, you can:

Install and manage software (including Python and R packages) along with all their dependencies
Create isolated environments so different projects can use different versions of the same software without conflicts.
Reproduce analyses easily since an environment file (environment.yml) can capture the exact software versions used in your workflow.

This makes conda especially useful for computational biology and single-cell RNA-seq analysis where software dependencies can be complex and version-sensitive.






NoteConda vs renv (Python vs R origins)



Although Conda was originally popularized for Python workflows, it can also manage R environments (and many non-Python tools).
However, for R-specific projects, many users prefer renv for its tight integration with R and CRAN/Bioconductor packages.
In mixed R + Python projects, a common pattern is:

Use renv to lock down R packages for the R portion of the analysis, and
Use conda (or another Python environment manager) for Python dependencies (often accessed from R via reticulate). If your workflow includes both R and Python components, you may find it helpful to use both tools in tandem. To learn more about renv, see our renv tutorial.









NoteInstalling Conda



To install Conda, follow the Miniconda installation instructions.
Miniconda is the lightweight version of Anaconda and is recommended for most users.

title: Creating a Conda Environment
text:
Create a New Environment
There are many ways to organize your Conda environments, depending on your workflow and software dependencies. As a general rule, you should create a dedicated Conda environment for each project to prevent version conflicts and ensure reproducibility.
To create a conda environment with Python, type into the command line:
conda create --name myenv
and replace myenv with your desired environment name. Then, to activate your environment:
conda activate myenv
Now, anything you run in this shell will use only the packages that you have explicitly installed. To deactivate your environment, run:
conda deactivate

title: Creating a Conda Environment
text:
Installing a Package with Conda
To install a package with Conda, you usually have to specify a channel — an online repository where packages are stored and distributed. When you install a package with Conda, it looks for that package in one or more channels (for example, defaults, conda-forge, or bioconda).

defaults — maintained by Anaconda; includes general-purpose packages.
conda-forge - a community-maintained channel providing up-to-date, cross-platform scientific software.
bioconda — specializes in bioinformatics software and builds on conda-forge for its dependencies.

When installing bioinformatics tools like kb-python or seqspec, it’s best practice to specify both conda-forge and bioconda as channels to ensure compatibility:
conda install -c conda-forge -c bioconda kb-python

title: Creating a Conda Environment
section: How to Save and Restore an Old Environment
text:
How to Save and Restore an Old Environment
You can save your conda environment in a YAML file with:
conda env export --from-history > environment.yml
The --from-history flag saves only the packages you explicitly installed, keeping the file concise. If you prefer to include all dependencies and versions, omit this flag.
To recreate the same environment elsewhere:
conda env create -f environment.yml --name myenv

title: Creating a Conda Environment
section: Using Conda Environments with JupyterLab and Jupyter Notebooks
text:
Using Conda Environments with JupyterLab and Jupyter Notebooks
Most Scanpy workflows are run interactively in JupyterLab or Jupyter notebooks. To use a conda environment in JupyterLab, you need to register it as a kernel. This allows you to select that environment when creating or running notebooks.

Registering a conda environment as a Jupyter kernel
First, from your project environment:
conda activate myenv
conda install -c conda-forge ipykernel
python -m ipykernel install --user --name myenv --display-name "Python (myenv)"
Then start JupyterLab from wherever you normally run it. The Python (myenv) kernel will still be available.


Quick sanity checks
Inside a notebook cell, confirm you are using the expected environment:
import sys
print(sys.executable)
You can also confirm that Scanpy is being imported from the environment you expect:
import scanpy as sc
print(sc.__version__)


Removing a kernel (optional)
If you no longer need the kernel registration:
jupyter kernelspec list
jupyter kernelspec uninstall myenv

title: Installing FastQC
text:
Install FastQC






NoteInstalling by OS



This tutorial uses general installation commands that apply across operating systems. For detailed, OS-specific instructions, see the official FastQC installation guide.


The entire FastQC program is distributed as a standalone zip archive. Download the latest version (v0.12.1 at the time of writing) from the official FastQC website.
Once downloaded, navigate to the directory containing the file and run:
# Unzip file
tar -xf /path/to/fastqc_v0.12.1.zip

# Make fastqc executable
chmod 755 /path/to/FastQC/fastqc

# (Optional) Make a symbolic link so you can run FASTQC from anywhere
sudo ln -s /path/to/FastQC/fastqc /usr/local/bin/fastqc






ImportantSudo Access



If you don’t have sudo privileges on your machine, ask your system administrator to install FastQC for you. Otherwise, you’ll need to call the FastQC executable directly using a relative path each time:
./path/to/FastQC/fastqc ...
rather than simply:
fastqc ...

text:
Step 2: Alter the Sequence Spec

Update the Read Objects
10x Genomics recommends that you sequence Read 2 to at least 90 nucleotides, but you may have decided to sequence to up to 150 nucleotides. If that is the case, you can modify the length of Read 2 by changing the min_len and max_len to reflect the read length. For example, if you want to sequence 150 nucleotides, update the read as follows:
- !Read
  read_id: read_2
  name: Read 2
  modality: rna
  primer_id: truseq_read2
  min_len: 150
  max_len: 150
  strand: neg
  files:
  - !File
    ...
You may have also decided not to sequence the sample indices if you did not sequence multiple samples at once. In this case, you should remove the Index 1 and Index 2 read objects.


Add in the file objects
Next, for each read, you should fill out a file object referencing the corresponding file.
A file object in seqspec if formatted as follows:
- !File
      file_id: 
      filename:
      filetype: 
      filesize: 
      url: 
      urltype: 
      md5: 
file_id, filetype, filename — identifies and describes the file
filesize - the size of the file
url - (optional) the link to the file
urltype - (optional) the type of url
md5 - (optional) MD5 fingerprint of data






NoteWhat does md5 stand for?



An MD5 checksum is a short, unique string of letters and numbers generated from a file’s contents. It acts like a digital fingerprint — if even a single byte in the file changes, the checksum will be different.
Including the MD5 value for your files allows others to verify data integrity after download or transfer, ensuring that the file has not been corrupted or altered.
To generate an MD5 checksum string, type into your command-line:
md5sum <filename> # For Linux

md5 <filename> # For macOS

Get-FileHash -Algorithm MD5 -Path <filename> # For Windows

title: seqspec Tutorial: 10X Chromium Next GEM Single Cell 3’ v3.1 Dual-Indexed Library
text:
Step 3: Alter the Library Spec
You can modify the min_len and max_len values for the cDNA region to reflect your desired read length. For example, if you want to sequence 150 nucleotides of cDNA, update the region as follows:
    - !Region
        parent_id: rna
        region_id: cdna
        region_type: cdna
        name: cDNA
        sequence_type: random
        sequence: XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
        min_len: 150
        max_len: 150
        onlist: null
        regions: null
Adjust the number of X characters in the sequence field to match the new length, and then adjust the full library sequence and length accordingly.
library_spec: 
- !Region
  region_id: rna
  region_type: named
  name: rna
  sequence_type: joined
  sequence: 
  min_len: 314
  max_len: 314
  onlist: null
  parent_id: null
  regions:
  - !Region
    ...

Now you have a complete seqspec file for your data. All that’s left is to save it with your dataset!
